Headline: As a senior engineering leader, I enjoy creating effective, world-class engineering teams empowered to solve big problems. I give my team autonomy in how they solve problems, allowing them to help guide us in the “how” while staying focused on the overall vision. After more than ten years in startups and 20 years in the tech industry, it has become critical for me to guide teams to deliver solutions quickly and with the flexibility to find the initial solution by following our users into a complete application.   The key is that engineering teams have to provide impact or value. Aligning with the product management and executive team on the most important work is critical. My teams have done this repeatedly, creating 100’s of millions of dollars of new revenue, bringing many new users to the platform, and opening up billion-dollar new lines of business.  I pride myself on being able to recruit top engineers to join me in my endeavors to build software that benefits everyone who uses it. I have grown small teams at WP Engine, Bazaarvoice, and Indeed.com to large, productive powerhouses that are creating new value far beyond the cost of the group. I also delight in the individual growth of my team members as they progress in their careers.  As an Engineering leader, I establish the vision for new ways of working. At Indeed.com, we created an internal venture capital system that allowed us to rapidly explore new product innovation in a cost-sensitive and time-efficient manner. We launched over a dozen new products from our Incubator into the broader Indeed.com ecosystem, and many of those projects are the backbone of the successes Indeed sees today. While at Bazaarvoice, I led the team to build the first genuinely software-as-a-service application. In doing so, we took the revenue from that product and expanded it 20X to hundreds of millions of dollars.  Ultimately, the goal of all of this is to make the world a better place. Whether that’s helping engineers, consumers, businesses, homeowners, job seekers, employers, or some other group, I’m excited when we’re participating in social good. I’ve led B2B, B2C, SaaS, Support, and practically every other type of engineering team out there, and when you get that mission right, it makes all the difference.

We buy, build, and scale SaaS companies with precision, speed, and zero corporate nonsense. Yes, we work with AI. No, I don’t need to drown you in AI-vomit to prove it. We acquire vertical SaaS companies that have potential buried under years of neglect — and we turn them into high-performance businesses.  And here’s the part people underestimate: I don’t do this alone.  My partner Eric Spielman is a force of nature on the revenue, operations, and customer side. He’s the one who walks into messy organizations and instantly sees how to unlock growth, streamline processes, and get teams aligned. He understands customers in a way most product people pretend to. He knows how to sell, how to scale, and how to operate at a level that frankly scares underprepared founders.  Eric handles the outside. I handle the inside. Together, we turn SaaS companies into something far more valuable than what we bought.  At our flagship, FranFunnel, I run product, engineering, infrastructure, and the entire AI stack — from messaging pipelines and backend architecture to intelligence-driven workflows. If it touches tech, performance, data, or automation, I own it. If it affects revenue, scale, or go-to-market, Eric owns it. It’s a clean, deadly partnership — no bureaucracy, no confusion, no wasted motion.  My approach to AI is ruthlessly practical: 	•	If it solves a real problem, deploy it. 	•	If it’s fluff, delete it. 	•	If someone suggests an AI feature “because everyone else is doing it,” I suggest they find a new playground.  AI is leverage — not an identity.  Under the hood, I’m hands-on across Python, Node, React/React Native, Postgres, BigQuery, Datastream, Twilio, Docker/Kubernetes, Salesforce integrations, and whatever else is required to make a SaaS platform feel effortless to customers and brutal to competitors. I lead technology operations in fintech — the disciplines that ensure both our platform and our people have what they need to perform, scale, and stay protected.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      In a high-availability, highly transactional environment, uptime isn't a metric. It's a promise — to every client on the platform and to every employee who depends on the tools and infrastructure that run the business. My teams own both sides of that equation: the production operations that keep client-facing services reliable, and the IT services to enable the business and that keep the organization productive and secure.  On the platform side, that means: - Defining what "production ready" actually requires — not just code deployed, but services that are monitored, documented, supported, and measured against SLOs - Building release governance that scales — aligning engineering, product, security, compliance, support, and client-facing teams around a shared standard for what we launch - Leading incident management and operational health programs that reduce time to resolution and prevent recurrence through systemic correction, not just heroics  On the IT side, that means: - Delivering reliable, secure end-user services — infrastructure, collaboration, endpoint management, and identity and access — that let teams do their best work without friction - Aligning corporate technology decisions with security posture, compliance requirements, and business growth - Managing the vendor and tooling landscape with a focus on consolidation, cost discipline, and strategic fit  Across both, the through-line is the same: operational maturity, service accountability, and building teams and frameworks that make reliability a system — not a personality trait.  I operate at the intersection of technology strategy and operational execution — where decisions about how we build and how we run directly shape how the business performs and how clients experience trust.

Sheriff’s offices, police departments, emergency management agencies, and organizations that support older adults across the U.S. use ConfirmOK to proactively check on seniors and vulnerable residents living alone.Enrolled residents get a call at a time they choose.
